scripts.title: Why ads don't work (you're doing it wrong)
scripts.film: Film Marco sitting down, relaxed, direct eye contact with camera. Natural lighting, neutral background. Lean in slightly when making the key point about expectations.

captions.label: A
captions.text: ads don't fail because they don't work. they fail because you're expecting the wrong thing from them.

captions.label: B
captions.text: most service business owners kill their ad campaigns in the first 2-3 weeks because they don't understand what they're actually buying. spoiler: you're not buying instant leads. you're buying data.

captions.label: C
captions.text: you can't expect week one results from week one spend.

lines.text: I talk to a lot of service business owners who tell me ads don't work for them. And almost every time, they killed the campaign way too early.

Here's what happens. You turn on ads, you spend some money, and in the first week or two you get maybe one or two leads. Or you're not happy with the cost per lead. So you shut it off and say, yeah, ads don't work.

But what you're actually doing is buying data. You're teaching the algorithm who your customer is. It takes time — usually weeks, sometimes a couple months — for it to actually find the right people at the right cost. You're not paying for leads in week one. You're paying for learning.

The owners I work with who see real results? They give it time. They let the algorithm do its job. And then somewhere down the line, boom — suddenly the cost per lead drops, the volume picks up, and it actually becomes profitable. But you have to be patient enough to get there.
Ads don't fail because they're broken. They fail because expectations don't match reality.

scripts.title: Why some service businesses scale and others don't
scripts.film: Film Marco sitting down, relaxed, direct eye contact. Natural lighting. One casual hand gesture when explaining the two paths — nothing overdone. Confidence but conversational.

captions.label: A
captions.text: the businesses that scale have one thing in common — they treat their ads like a real business system, not a side experiment

captions.label: B
captions.text: scaling service businesses do three things different: they commit to consistent spend, they actually track what's working, and they hire for it. stuck ones do none of that.

captions.label: C
captions.text: scale or stay stuck — usually comes down to whether you treat ads like a cost or an investment

lines.text: I've watched plumbers, electricians, window cleaners scale to multiple trucks. And I've watched others do the exact same ads and stay flat. The difference isn't the ads. It's how they think about them.

The ones that scale? They commit. They don't test with what's left over after payroll. They actually allocate real ad spend because they know that's how you build predictable lead flow. The stuck ones? They turn ads on when work is slow and off when it's busy. That doesn't work.

Second thing — they track. They know their cost per lead, their close rate, what month performs best. Stuck businesses run ads and hope. They don't actually know if it's working or why. So they can't improve it.

Third — they don't try to run it themselves forever. At some point they hire someone or work with an agency to manage it. They treat it like a real part of their business, not a side project they'll figure out someday.
Scale isn't luck. It's consistency, measurement, and treating growth like the business it is.

scripts.title: Why service business owners quit ads too early
scripts.film: Screen record Meta Ads Manager dashboard showing campaign performance over time. Click through to show metrics like CTR, CPC, and conversion data across weeks 1-4. Slow, deliberate clicks—let people read the numbers. Keep cursor visible and intentional. Speak naturally as you navigate.

captions.label: A
captions.text: most service business owners look at week one data and assume it's broken. it's not.

captions.label: B
captions.text: your first week of ads is data collection, not performance. the real story shows up in week 3-4. if you quit too early, you never see it.

captions.label: C
captions.text: your ad account needs time to learn what actually works.

lines.text: here's why your ads probably felt like a waste of money—you quit before they even had a chance to work.
when you launch a campaign, Meta's algorithm is literally learning. it doesn't know yet who to show your ads to, what time gets you leads, which placements actually convert. that's weeks one and two.

so yeah, your first week looks rough. cost per lead is high. click-through rate's soft. but that's the cost of teaching Meta how to find your customers. most owners see that and kill the campaign right there.
by week three and four, if there's real demand in your market, the algorithm has figured it out. your cost per lead drops. your quality improves. but you have to let it get there first.
give it three weeks before you decide if ads work for you. that's not a guarantee—but it's the difference between quitting too early and actually seeing what's possible.

scripts.title: The hook formula that actually works
scripts.film: Film Marco sitting down, casual setup, natural lighting. Direct eye contact with camera. Relaxed but engaged — like he's explaining something he's figured out to a friend. No background clutter.

captions.label: A
captions.text: every service business ad needs a hook that speaks to the problem, not the solution

captions.label: B
captions.text: hooks work when they nail the pain point first. plumbers, hvac, cleaners, detailers — same formula applies. lead with the problem your customer actually has.

captions.label: C
captions.text: lead with the problem. everything else follows.

lines.text: most service business ads fail because they start with the wrong thing. and it's not what you think.
you're probably starting with what you do. like, 'we're your local plumber' or 'professional window cleaning.' but your customer doesn't care about that yet. they care about their problem.

the hook that works is the one that makes someone go, 'oh, that's me.' you lead with the pain point. the broken pipe at midnight. the dirty gutters before the rainy season. the car that hasn't been detailed in months.
once you've got their attention with the problem, then you show the solution. that's when you talk about what you do. but the hook? it's always about them first, not you.
nail the problem in the first three seconds, and the rest of the ad writes itself.

scripts.title: Real job photos beat stock images 3x
scripts.film: Film Marco in his workspace or home office, relaxed. When explaining the comparison, have a phone or tablet visible showing side-by-side examples of a stock photo vs. a real job photo from an actual client. Keep energy conversational and genuine.

captions.label: A
captions.text: your actual job photos convert way better than anything staged. people can smell stock photography from a mile away.

captions.label: B
captions.text: here's what i see: plumbers using generic contractor stock images get half the clicks of guys running their real before-and-afters. authenticity wins every single time.

captions.label: C
captions.text: stop paying for stock photos when your best ad creative is literally your last job site.

lines.text: i just checked a client's account and swapped out their stock photo ads for real job photos. their click rate went from 2% to almost 6%. that's not luck — that's trust.

people scrolling Facebook know a stock image when they see one. it screams generic. but when they see an actual before-and-after from a real job in your area, something clicks. they think that could be their house, their yard, their bathroom.

here's the simple part: take your phone, shoot a couple shots on your next job. the mess, the finished product, you standing next to it with dirty hands. that's your best creative right there. way more powerful than anything a design tool can fake.

your competitors are still using the same five stock photos everyone else is. you're running real work. you're literally showing people you do the job they're about to hire you for. that's the difference between a scroll and a call.
authenticity isn't just better for conversions. it's the only thing that actually builds trust in this space.

scripts.title: Why your ads die after 3 days
scripts.film: Film Marco sitting or standing in natural light, relaxed posture, speaking directly at camera like he's explaining something to a friend over coffee. Quick cuts to his face from slightly different angles if he shifts. Keep it simple and real — no fancy background needed.

captions.label: A
captions.text: your ad isn't broken. you're just showing it to the same people twice.

captions.label: B
captions.text: your ad works for 3 days then dies because you're exhausting your audience. solution: broader targeting or fresh creative every few days.

captions.label: C
captions.text: ad fatigue kills performance faster than you think — and it's preventable.

lines.text: So your ad's crushing it for three days, then just... dies. And you have no idea why. Here's what's actually happening.

You're showing the same ad to the same people over and over. That works the first time they see it. Second time, they barely notice. Third time? They're scrolling past or hitting that 'see less' button.
This is called ad fatigue. Your audience gets numb to the message. Your cost per lead goes up. Your conversion rate tanks. All because you're not rotating fresh creative or expanding who sees it.
The fix is simple: either broaden your audience so you're reaching new people, or swap out your creative every few days. Test a new angle, new copy, new hook. Keep them seeing something fresh.
Most business owners don't realize ad fatigue is happening until the numbers tell them. But now you do. Move faster than the algorithm does.

scripts.title: Retargeting gets you the cheapest leads
scripts.film: Film Marco sitting casually, natural lighting, slightly leaned in — like he's explaining something he's figured out. Direct eye contact with camera. Maybe a slight head tilt when making the core point.

captions.label: A
captions.text: people who already know you convert way cheaper than cold traffic. that's the whole game right there.

captions.label: B
captions.text: cold traffic is expensive because they don't know who you are. retargeting? they visited your site already. they're ready. cheaper cost per lead. way better ROI.

captions.label: C
captions.text: retargeting to people who already visited you costs 60-70% less than finding new people.

lines.text: If you're not retargeting your website visitors, you're literally throwing money away on the most expensive leads possible.

Here's the thing — cold traffic costs a fortune because those people have no idea who you are. They don't know your work, they don't trust you yet, so Meta has to show your ad a bunch of times before anyone clicks.

But retargeting? Those are people who already visited your website. They saw your work, your prices, your reviews. They're warm. They're way more likely to book or call. And you're paying way less per lead because the conversion intent is already there.

Most business owners spend all their budget chasing cold traffic and barely touch retargeting. It's backwards. You should be hitting those warm visitors hard because they're the ones who actually turn into jobs.
Retargeting isn't just cheaper — it's smarter money. Do that first, then scale cold traffic once you've got it dialed in.

scripts.title: Why service owners quit ads too early
scripts.film: Film in your office or workspace, natural lighting, sit or stand at a comfortable distance. Lean in slightly during the hook and Point 1 to show intensity. Stay relaxed and conversational throughout — this is you explaining something you've seen happen a hundred times.

captions.label: A
captions.text: the ads aren't broken. your expectations were.

captions.label: B
captions.text: most service business owners expect results in week 2. real momentum takes 4-6 weeks of consistent spend and testing. know the timeline before you start.

captions.label: C
captions.text: ads don't fail — patience does.

lines.text: I see this all the time. A plumber or window cleaner starts running ads, gets excited, then kills the campaign after three weeks because they're not seeing enough leads yet.
Here's the thing — Meta's algorithm needs time to learn. The first two weeks, it's literally still figuring out who your customer is. You're feeding it data. That's not failure, that's the setup.

Most owners expect results in week two and panic in week three. By week four or five, when the algorithm actually starts performing, they've already pulled the plug. So they never see the actual breakthrough.
If your budget is real and your offer is solid, give it at least four to six weeks of consistent spend before you decide if it's working. That's just how this works.
The owners who scale aren't smarter. They just had the patience to let the system actually work.

scripts.title: The campaign objective mistake costing you leads
scripts.film: Film sitting down, casual setting like an office or home workspace. Look directly at camera. Lean in slightly when making the main point. Keep energy steady and conversational — like you're explaining something to a peer over coffee.

captions.label: A
captions.text: most service businesses are set up to optimize for clicks instead of actual calls and bookings. that's the gap.

captions.label: B
captions.text: you see a lot of plumbers, hvac guys, and detailers running lead generation ads. but their campaigns are built to chase clicks, not conversions. when you're optimizing for the wrong thing, your ad spend gets spread thin and you end up paying more per actual job.

captions.label: C
captions.text: stop optimizing for clicks. start optimizing for calls and bookings.

lines.text: I see this all the time with service business owners — you're running ads, you're getting clicks, but your phone's not ringing. And the reason is usually your campaign objective is just... wrong.

Most people pick lead generation or traffic as their objective. That sounds right, but here's the thing — those are built to optimize for clicks and form fills. Not for actual calls. Not for bookings.

When Meta's algorithm is chasing clicks, it's finding people who click. But clickers aren't always callers. So you end up paying for a ton of low-intent traffic, your cost per call goes up, and you're spinning your wheels.

If you're a plumber, a window cleaner, a detailer — you don't need clicks. You need calls and booked appointments. So shift your objective to conversions and tie it to actual phone calls or booking confirmations. Let the algorithm find the people who actually convert.
Small change, huge difference in results.

scripts.title: Why retargeting is your cheapest lead source
scripts.film: Screen record inside Meta Ads Manager. Start on a campaign dashboard showing a retargeting campaign with strong metrics. Navigate to the Audiences section, show a retargeting audience size. Then move to Results comparing a cold traffic campaign next to a retargeting campaign — highlight the cost per result difference. Keep it calm, zoom in on key numbers, speak naturally over it.

captions.label: A
captions.text: people who already know you cost way less to convert. retargeting is just reminding them you exist.

captions.label: B
captions.text: retargeting works because they've already seen your business. they just need one more reason to call. that costs less than finding brand new people from scratch.

captions.label: C
captions.text: cheapest leads come from people who already visited your site. retargeting is the money move.

lines.text: If you're only running cold traffic ads, you're leaving your cheapest leads on the table.
See this? This is a cold campaign — new people who've never heard of your business. Cost per lead is solid, but it takes work to convince a stranger.
Now look at retargeting. Same business, same offer. But these are people who already visited your site, saw your work, maybe even scrolled through your portfolio. They're already interested.
Cost per lead drops because you're not convincing anymore — you're just reminding. They just need that one extra touch to pick up the phone. Way cheaper than hunting cold.
The best leads you'll ever pay for are the ones who already know you. Retargeting is how you turn website visitors into actual customers.

scripts.title: Why your lead cost keeps climbing
scripts.film: Film Marco sitting down, relaxed, looking directly at camera. Natural light. Maybe in an office or home setting. Lean in slightly when explaining the mistakes — like he's giving advice to a friend across the table.

captions.label: A
captions.text: your lead cost isn't high because of facebook. it's high because your ads are talking to the wrong people.

captions.label: B
captions.text: most service business owners think higher lead cost means they need a better platform or cheaper ads. actually it means your targeting is off, your landing page isn't converting, or you're not pre-qualifying leads before they hit your funnel.

captions.label: C
captions.text: lead cost is a symptom. the real problem is usually one of three things — and it's fixable.

lines.text: Most service business owners think their lead cost is high because the platform's getting expensive. That's not what's happening.

Usually it's one of three things. First — your targeting is too broad. You're paying to reach people who'll never need your service. A plumber paying to reach renters in apartments with no plumbing issues, a window cleaner reaching people in a market with two-week lead times already booked. Tighten it up.

Second — your landing page or ad creative isn't converting. You're getting clicks but no real leads. The message doesn't match what people actually want to hear. An HVAC company talking about energy efficiency when homeowners just want their AC working today. Fix the messaging first.

Third — you're not pre-qualifying. Every single person who fills out a form becomes a lead cost, even the ones who aren't ready, can't afford you, or are just shopping around. Add qualification questions. Filter before they hit your inbox.
Lead cost isn't a platform problem. It's a targeting, conversion, or qualification problem. Fix those and everything changes.

scripts.title: Structure Your First Meta Ad Campaign Right
scripts.film: Film Marco sitting casually, well-lit, direct to camera. Relaxed posture. Can use hand gestures to emphasize the three points. Background should be clean but natural — office or home setup is fine.

captions.label: A
captions.text: most people skip the foundation and wonder why their ads don't work. structure matters more than the budget.

captions.label: B
captions.text: your first campaign isn't about winning. it's about learning what works for your specific market. nail the three basics and everything else gets easier.

captions.label: C
captions.text: campaign structure is where most service business owners leave money on the table without even knowing it.

lines.text: Most service business owners launch their first Meta campaign and wonder why it doesn't work. Here's the thing — it's not the platform. It's how you structure it from the start.

First thing — separate your audiences. You're not running one campaign to everyone. You need one for cold traffic, people who've never heard of you. And a separate one for warm traffic, people who already know you exist. They convert different. They need different messaging.

Second — your ad sets matter. Don't just pick one creative and hope it works. Test at least three different angles. Show your work, show results, show the problem you solve. Let Meta figure out which one resonates. It always works better with options.

Third — track what actually matters. Don't just watch impressions or clicks. You need to know which campaign is bringing you actual booked jobs or qualified leads. That's it. Everything else is noise.
Get the structure right first. The money follows.

scripts.title: Boosted Post vs Real Meta Ad Campaign
scripts.film: Film Marco sitting at a desk or standing casual, relaxed energy. Use hand gestures to show the difference — maybe show phone screen briefly if needed. Keep it conversational and direct to camera.

captions.label: A
captions.text: boosted posts get your existing audience. real campaigns find new customers who actually need your service.

captions.label: B
captions.text: most service business owners think boosting a post is running ads. it's not. here's why that matters for your lead generation.

captions.label: C
captions.text: if you're just boosting posts, you're leaving money on the table.

lines.text: Most plumbers, electricians, and detailers I talk to think boosting a post is the same thing as running a Meta ad campaign. It's not. And that difference is costing you leads.

When you boost a post, you're basically just pushing your existing content to people who already know you or follow you. It's cheap, it's fast, and it feels like you're doing ads. But you're mostly showing up in front of people who already like your page.

A real campaign is different. You're building custom audiences based on who actually needs your service — people searching for plumbers in your area, people looking at HVAC companies, people who've shown intent. You're targeting the right person at the right time with the right message.

Boosted posts are fine if you just want engagement. But if you want leads that turn into jobs? You need a real campaign with proper targeting, conversion tracking, and optimization. The difference in results is night and day.
Start thinking about your ads like a fishing rod, not a megaphone. You want precision, not noise.

scripts.title: Lead form setup that actually converts

overlay.line2: The right way to set it up

scripts.film: Film Marco sitting down casually, natural lighting, relaxed posture. No desk clutter. Direct eye contact with camera throughout. Film in a clean, professional but lived-in space — feels like advice from someone who's actually done this.

captions.label: A
captions.text: most service businesses set up lead forms wrong. here's what actually works.

captions.label: B
captions.text: your lead form is leaving money on the table if you're asking too many questions. less fields = more conversions = more leads for your business.

captions.label: C
captions.text: three fields. that's it. name, phone, and one question. everything else kills your form.

lines.text: Most service business owners are tanking their conversion rate the second someone clicks their Meta ad. And it's because of their lead form setup.
Here's the thing — every field you add to a form drops your completion rate. I'm talking like 10 to 15 percent per extra question. So you've got to be ruthless about what you actually need.

Start with three fields maximum. Name, phone number, and one contextual question. For a plumber that might be 'what's the issue?' For a detailer it's 'what service?' That's it. You can ask the rest during the call.
Also make sure your thank you page actually does something. Send them to a page that confirms the appointment or gives them your number to call. Don't just say 'thanks.' Give them a next step.
Fewer fields, better data, more actual leads calling you. That's how you scale without wasting ad spend on junk submissions.

Here's what happens. You turn on ads, you spend some money, and in the first week or two you get maybe one or two leads. Or you're not happy with the cost per lead. So you shut it off and say, yeah, ads don't work.

But what you're actually doing is buying data. You're teaching the algorithm who your customer is. It takes time — usually weeks, sometimes a couple months — for it to actually find the right people at the right cost. You're not paying for leads in week one. You're paying for learning.

The owners I work with who see real results? They give it time. They let the algorithm do its job. And then somewhere down the line, boom — suddenly the cost per lead drops, the volume picks up, and it actually becomes profitable. But you have to be patient enough to get there.
Ads don't fail because they're broken. They fail because expectations don't match reality.

lines.text: If you're not retargeting your website visitors, you're literally throwing money away on the most expensive leads possible.

Here's the thing — cold traffic costs a fortune because those people have no idea who you are. They don't know your work, they don't trust you yet, so Meta has to show your ad a bunch of times before anyone clicks.

But retargeting? Those are people who already visited your website. They saw your work, your prices, your reviews. They're warm. They're way more likely to book or call. And you're paying way less per lead because the conversion intent is already there.

Most business owners spend all their budget chasing cold traffic and barely touch retargeting. It's backwards. You should be hitting those warm visitors hard because they're the ones who actually turn into jobs.
Retargeting isn't just cheaper — it's smarter money. Do that first, then scale cold traffic once you've got it dialed in.
